did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a custom Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) for multi-class skin cancer classification on dermoscopic images from the ISIC-2018 and ISIC-2019 datasets. It also employs interpretable AI methods, specifically LIME and SHAP, to explain the model's predictions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, including the development of a custom Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) for multi-class skin cancer classification, the use of data augmentation, and evaluation on ISIC-2018 and ISIC-2019 datasets. It reports specific performance metrics (e.g., 96.37% accuracy) and compares results with baseline models, which characterizes it as a research paper rather than a review.

title: MSCC: Multi-Class Skin Cancer Classification and Interpretable Deep Learning Systems
journal: 2024 6th International Conference on Electrical Engineering and Information & Communication Technology (ICEEICT)
brief_summary: This study introduces a multi-class skin cancer classification (MSCC) system utilizing a custom Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) to distinguish between seven types of skin lesions. The model was trained and evaluated on merged ISIC-2018 and ISIC-2019 dermoscopic image datasets, employing data augmentation to address class imbalance. The proposed DCNN achieved an average accuracy of 96.37%, along with precision, recall, and F1 scores averaging 96.39%, 96.35%, and 96.36%, respectively. These results surpassed four baseline classifiers, including VGG-16, InceptionV3, ResNet50, and MobileNet. To enhance interpretability, the study integrated LIME and SHAP frameworks to generate visual explanations for the model's predictions. The findings demonstrate that the proposed system offers high accuracy and transparency, making it suitable for clinical decision support in early skin cancer detection.

all_task_types.target: Seven distinct types of skin lesions (benign keratosis lesions, melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, melanocytic nevi, vascular lesions, actinic keratosis, and dermatofibroma)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class skin cancer classification using a custom Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) on dermoscopic images from ISIC-2018 and ISIC-2019 datasets. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories for seven lesion classes, achieving 96.37% accuracy, 96.39% precision, 96.35% recall, and 96.36% F1-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dermoscopic image dataset containing 7 types of skin lesions. Merged with ISIC-2019 for this study.

use.training: Merged with ISIC-2019 and used for training the DCNN model after data augmentation.
use.validation: Merged with ISIC-2019 and used for validation of the DCNN model.
use.testing: Merged with ISIC-2019 and used for testing/evaluation of the DCNN model.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2019
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dermoscopic image dataset containing 8 types of skin lesions. Merged with ISIC-2018 for this study.

use.training: Merged with ISIC-2018 and used for training the DCNN model after data augmentation.
use.validation: Merged with ISIC-2018 and used for validation of the DCNN model.
use.testing: Merged with ISIC-2018 and used for testing/evaluation of the DCNN model.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC-2018 and ISIC-2019 (Merged).notes: The proposed model was evaluated on a merged dataset of ISIC-2018 and ISIC-2019. It outperformed the baseline models (VGG-16, InceptionV3, ResNet50, MobileNet) across all metrics. The training was halted at 100 epochs as performance plateaued after 20 epochs. The study also utilized LIME and SHAP for interpretability.

architecture_details.description: Structured with repeated layers of convolution, pooling, dropout, flattening, and dense layers. Convolutional layers use varying kernel sizes and filter depths. MaxPooling provides spatial down-sampling. Dropout mitigates overfitting.

training_details.notes: Training halted at 100 epochs as performance plateaued after 20 epochs.

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer with softmax activation
classification_or_output_head.output: Probabilities for categorizing skin cancer into seven unique classes

preprocessing_techniques: Data cleaning to address NULL values and inconsistencies
Dermoscopic hair removal techniques to eliminate artifacts
Data augmentation (rotation, flipping, cropping, and zooming) to address class imbalance
Histogram equalization to improve contrast and feature visibility
Normalization to standardize pixel values for training stability

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rotation, flipping, cropping, and zooming were applied to address class imbalance and promote fair representation of each class.

Data augmentation was used to generate composite samples for each class to mitigate class imbalance. Specific post-augmentation counts per class are reported in Table I (e.g., Melanoma increased from 1,113 to 6,349 in ISIC-2018; Vascular lesions increased from 142 to 6,103 in ISIC-2018).

conclusions: The proposed Multi-Class Skin Cancer Classification (MSCC) system, utilizing a custom Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN), achieved high performance with an average accuracy of 96.37%, precision of 96.39%, recall of 96.35%, and F1 score of 96.36% on merged ISIC-2018 and ISIC-2019 datasets.
The proposed DCNN model outperformed four baseline classifiers (VGG-16, InceptionV3, ResNet50, and MobileNet) across all evaluated metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score).
Data augmentation techniques were effective in mitigating class imbalance issues prevalent in medical datasets, thereby improving overall model accuracy.

The integration of interpretable AI frameworks, specifically LIME and SHAP, enhanced model transparency and trustworthiness by providing visual explanations aligned with prior beliefs and adhering to best practices for general explanations.
The incorporation of explainability enhances the model's utility in real clinical scenarios, aiding in early detection and reducing the strain on healthcare professionals.

The study acknowledges limitations in providing accurate explanations for complex models and identifies future research directions including exploration of diverse datasets, healthcare settings, and integration of emerging technologies like federated learning and IoT to enhance generalizability.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges dataset bias inherent in the ISIC-2018 and ISIC-2019 datasets as a limitation, though it attempts to mitigate this via data augmentation.
The paper admits that the interpretability methods used (LIME and SHAP) have limitations in providing accurate explanations for complex models.
There is no external validation; the model was trained and evaluated solely on merged ISIC-2018 and ISIC-2019 datasets, limiting generalizability claims.
The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (p-values or confidence intervals) for the performance comparisons against baseline models.
The model was trained on dermoscopic images, which may limit clinical relevance for non-dermoscopic clinical photography without further adaptation.
Data augmentation was heavily relied upon to address class imbalance, particularly for rare classes like Vascular lesions and Dermatofibroma, which may introduce synthetic bias.
